title: Pumpkin scones
subtitle: Australian-style scones with roasted pumpkin and warm spice
excerpt: Australian pumpkin scones are a Queensland baking tradition: orange-fleshed roasted pumpkin replaces some of the liquid in a standard scone dough, adding colour, flavour, and a tender crumb that holds moisture better than a plain scone. Warmly spiced, slightly golden, good with butter.
sourceNotes: Pumpkin scones are most strongly associated with Queensland, Australia, where Dame Florence Bjelke-Petersen's version (simple and without eggs) became iconic through its widespread publication in Australian Women's Weekly and community cookbooks from the 1960s onwards. The formula of mashed pumpkin stirred into a flour-and-butter dough with warm spice is the established Australian domestic type. The recipe here synthesises this tradition, adapted for the butternut squash common in British kitchens.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze fully baked scones once cool. Thaw at room temperature for 1 hour or warm in a low oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best within 2 hours of baking. Pumpkin scones stay moist longer than plain scones — still good the next day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 200°C. Done when deep gold on top, the sides have opened slightly, and the base sounds hollow when tapped — 12 to 14 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Roasted pumpkin or butternut squash acts as both flavour and moisture source in a scone dough. Because it contains more water than butter alone, pumpkin scone dough will feel softer and slightly stickier than a plain scone dough — this is correct. Do not add more flour to compensate, or the scones will be dry. The water in the pumpkin creates steam in the oven, contributing to the rise and keeping the crumb tender.

content.text: prevents the most common mistake: the dough feels softer than expected, and the instinct is to add more flour. Resist it. The extra moisture is why pumpkin scones stay tender longer than plain ones.

attrs.prepNote: roasted until tender, flesh mashed and cooled to room temperature

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C conventional. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift the flour, baking powder, salt, cinnamon, and mixed spice into a bowl. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in the sugar. Add the cooled mashed pumpkin and milk and mix with a knife until the dough comes together — it will be softer than a plain scone dough.

Turn onto a well-floured surface and press gently to 2.5 cm thickness. Cut into rounds of 5 to 6 cm diameter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the lined tray and brush the tops with milk. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until deep gold on top, the sides have opened, and the base sounds hollow when tapped. Best eaten warm.
